This is the doc of that param:

```   
:type expiration: :class:`int`, :class:`long`, :class:`datetime.datetime`,
:class:`datetime.timedelta`
:param expiration: When the signed URL should expire.
```
which gets passed to `get_expiration_seconds` which is:
```python
def get_expiration_seconds(expiration):
    """Convert 'expiration' to a number of seconds in the future.

    :type expiration: int, long, datetime.datetime, datetime.timedelta
    :param expiration: When the signed URL should expire.

    :raises TypeError: When expiration is not an integer.

    :rtype: int
    :returns: a timestamp as an absolute number of seconds.
    """
    # If it's a timedelta, add it to `now` in UTC.
    if isinstance(expiration, datetime.timedelta):
        now = NOW().replace(tzinfo=_helpers.UTC)
        expiration = now + expiration

    # If it's a datetime, convert to a timestamp.
    if isinstance(expiration, datetime.datetime):
        micros = _helpers._microseconds_from_datetime(expiration)
        expiration = micros // 10**6

    if not isinstance(expiration, six.integer_types):
        raise TypeError('Expected an integer timestamp, datetime, or '
                        'timedelta. Got %s' % type(expiration))
    return expiration
```

You're after "the last version in the array that is active", right?

I think this will only work if all of the `is_active ==false` are right at the beginning, and will pick the wrong value if that's not the case.

It's also quite an unusual (and hard to follow) implementation, since:
* when iterating an array manually (ie not using lodash or similar), it's more common (and more readable) to use a `for` loop and break early, rather than the `while` usage here
* we want the last matching element, so we should be starting at the end of the array and working backwards, since we can break early
* ideally we shouldn't be hand-writing any of this, there are typically built-ins for these patterns (or at least polyfills in the form of lodash that can be used until we're happy to say an ES6 browser is required)

In fact, there is actually a lodash function that will do exactly what we're after:
https://lodash.com/docs#findLast

I think it would be good to also rename the variable `version` to something more accurately representing it's value, like `default_version`, `last_version`, or `most_recent_version` or similar. Moving the `XXX` comment below up to where this variable is set, would probably make things clearer too :-)

I agree that validation is large and complicated but let me disagree with some other points.

1. Sometimes duplication is necessary. I once work on a project where we had same permission set for posts and comments. So one guy from our team once decided "Hey, it is duplication, lets use one function for both", he refactor the code and half a year later when all code points to new function we had to add an additional permission just for posts and not for comments. Guess what happened :grinning: . Same thing is here: while most of the validation is for lists, it all could change at some point. We can require different validation for weekdays for example.

2. It is actually not re-parsing the file, but validate already extracted data. File is loaded and transformed to yaml by `LanguageDataLoader`

3. Initial purpose of this change was to provide easy readable way to define languages. XML is not that. While it is great for machine-communication, it is rather hard to read for a human. Also i am not sure if it is possible to define facet that would check that groups in replacement are properly correlated to the matching regex.

4. Informative message is just a bonus here, main purpose is to keep all language information consistent. I mean if changes in structure of languages are made it is better to make a proper changes in validator to be sure that all languages received this change and nothing was left out. But validator is actually the one part of code that changes least frequently.

5. Assertions in the code instead of aside validation is really bad idea: parsing code is complicated enough by itself, and validation code does not needed for work. It is best to have validation in separate place.

Someday we can refactor this class to tests suite, but it should be done much-much later on the roadmap. For now we should be considered very young project that does not know what waits around the corner. And to be prepared for the rapid changes we sometimes need messy code. At least until we support the majority of languages around the world and know more about how they treat dates and do not make unnecessary assumptions (like word tokens does not contain spaces or only consists of word-characters) and then there would be less surprises on the way and we can relax a bit :smiley: .

* There should never be white-space at the end of a file (e.g. blank line(s)). My editor (Vim) has automatic checking for this sort of thing, yours might too. What editor do you use?
* I've recently adopted PEP 257 https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0257/, which governs the formatting of docstrings. The short story is that class docstrings should appear like this:

  ```python
  class CT_BookmarkStart(BaseOxmlElement):
      """`w:bookmarkStart` element

      This is a further elaboration of the docstring, often not needed for custom element classes.
      """

      def ...(...):
          ...
  ```

  The first line must be short enough to fit in a line length of 77, (as all code here) The rest of the docstring, if present should appear separated by a single blank line. Then there should be a blank line between the docstring and the first definition (often `def __init__()` in non-element classes).

  This is another case where I haven't made the change globally, but respect the standard for all new work and when I'm editing a class (or sometimes just in the neighborhood, but never when there aren't other changes to the file).

`--allow-no-subscriptions` can be used even if you have subscriptions. The story is, `login` will visit every tenants you are member of. Let us say you have `[t1,t2]`, and there is a subscription `s1` in `t1`, but not any in the `t2`. W/o this flag, the login will only build an account for `t1\s1`, which makes sense because almost all commands need to have subscriptions. But for a few commands which doesn't need subscriptions, say those `ad` ones, users have opened the issue saying they like to run them on `t2`. 
I don't like to build up the tenant level accounts by default, because more likely people don't want it. That is particularly true for RBAC scenarios, that it is common people by mistake forgot to create the role assignment, causing subscription access is not built up. I am open to incoming user voices though, and can switch if needed.

> I don't think that shifting the burden on clearing framework maintained flags onto the charm improves things. 

I actually think it improves things a lot. It's important to reduce the amount of "magic" as much as possible. Moreover, the "split-brain" issue will exist as long as we require charmers to think about _both_ charms.reactive and the hook lifecycle while writing handlers.

Granted, people who are familiar with hooks will need to learn this mapping, but that is a one-time effort and not a big one if we explain the behavior clearly. Especially since this isn't that far from the hook paradigm; most hooks just signal that something changed. When the charms.reactive framework gets such a signal, it sets a flag.

Look at the conversations -> endpoints shift. Even though conversations are more powerful, many people got stuck on them because their behavior is different depending on what hook is running.  In order to use conversations successfully, you have to be an expert in how relationships and their hooks work. Endpoints provide a lot less functionality, but what they do provide is reliable and consistent. Regardless of the hook you're in, everything will work the same and you only need to understand the flags in order to be able to use them.

Conversations, just like with the `config.changed` flags, also have the issue that it's very easy to write wrong code that _seemingly_ works but has unexpected behavior in edge cases. If a handler reacts to multiple preconditions, one of them being a relation state, then your charm might- or might not crash depending on whether the other preconditions are true _before-_ or _after_ the relationship hook during which the relation state is set. It's the same with config changed, although not so dramatically: a handler reacting to `config.changed` might or might not run depending on whether its other preconditions match during the `config-changed` hook or after. Moreover, most of the times when a charm _does_ rely on this behavior, it's still incorrect. For example, if you want to restart a server only when the config changes _after_ the server is started:

```python
@when('config.set.x')
def start():
    # generate config
    # start server
    set_flag('server.running')

@when('config.changed.x', 'server.running')
def restart():
    # update config
    # restart server
```

This will restart the server immediately after the first start. This is a typical beginners mistake that is probably present in many charms used in production. An easy fix is to add `clear_flag('config.changed.x')` to the `start` handler, but then we're already manually clearing these flags.. So just quit the magic, even if it means writing one more line of code..

> Why do we strip the ending '/' only if the path is not base_url + '/'?

Because the core plugin handles route `/`, which will become route `/foo/` with a path prefix of `/foo`. It's quite hacky. I think I'd prefer to just remove all trailing slashes everywhere internally (i.e., in the `self.data_applications` keyset). Then `_clean_path` is just `lambda path: path.rstrip('/')`. 

With the current semantics, using regex here is certainly overkill. Note that you'd need to actually write the pattern as `re.escape(path_prefix) + r'…'`. But there's no reason to use a regex engine at all when all you need are some simple string functions.

With the current semantics, I favor the implementation written in this PR, with the minor request to change `len(path) > 1` to `path != '/'`, so that the intent is clearer.

%ml train (in datalab code as a separate change) basically runs the function through inspection. The function is not supposed to be called directly.

There are a few reasons that a function is more flexible than program args:

1. Function arguments can easily be enumerated programmatically (so --dumpargs is easy to implement)
2. It can display package specific content in Datalab directly. For example, predict() can display image in output along with labels. preprocess() can display html links for DataFlow jobs. train() can display job logs. The output behavior can be different package by package.
3. There can be extra processing before submitting to cloud. In this case, we get the number of labels from labels file. We can also do more (such as validating package specific args).
